content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 
1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
1/ (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Scott Murtishaw 
1355 Berkeley Way 
Berkeley, CA 94702 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-082 
Dear Mr. Murtishaw: 
June 5, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding post-government employment 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section I 090. 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby ( 1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
As the former Energy Policy Adviser to the president of the California Public Utilities 
Commission ("CPUC"), are you prohibited under the permanent ban from acting as a consultant on 
matters related to the proceedings on Distribution Resource Plans (Rulemaking 14-08-013 ), and 
Integrated Distributed Energy Resources (Ruiemaking 14-10-003 )? 
CONCLUSION 
To the extent that these proceedings focused on the quasi-legislative matters of establishing 
policies, rules and methodologies of general application to the class of large electric investor-owned 
utilities, you are not barred by the permanent ban from consultant work related to these 
proceedings. The permanent ban does bar your participation in matters that do not apply equally to 
the class of utilities, to the extent you previously participated in that aspect of the rulemaking 
proceeding. 
1 The Polilical Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTOR 
File No. A-18-082 
Page No. 2 
You were formerly employed as Energy Policy Adviser to the president of the California 
Public Utilities Commission ("CPUC") from July 2010 until January 2018. While at the CPUC you 
worked on two rulemaking proceedings related to the relatively new area of Distributed Energy 
Resources" ("DERs").2 The two rulemaking proceedings have some overlap, and together work to 
ensure that cost-effective DERs are integrated by large investor-owned utility companies into the 
electric system's operations and distribution planning as an alternative to investments in traditional 
forms of electricity generation. 
Both proceedings addressed policy issues and methodology development, and you state that 
these proceedings are equally applicable to each member of the class of large electric investor-
owned utilities. The large investor-owned utilities are: Pacific Gas and Electric, Southern California 
Edison and San Diego Gas and Electric.3 
Public Utilities Code Section 769 was enacted in 2013 and required investor-owned utilities 
to file distribution resources plan ("DRP") proposals for the deployment of DERs.4 The CPU C's 
role is to review, modify, and approve each DRP proposal. However, little was set in place when 
the law was enacted as to what the DRP proposals should contain. 
R11/e111aki11g 14-08-013: Policies, Procedures a11d R11/esfor Distrib11tio11 Resources Plans 
("DRPs'? 
The CPUC iriitiated Rulemaking 14-08-013 as a quasi-legislative proceeding to provide 
guidance to the investor-owned utilities. The CPUC noted in the proceeding that over sixteen 
questions related to the development of DRPs were put forward for comment, with over thirty 
parties responding. A guidance ruling was issued in 2015, and the DRP proposals became 
"applications" subject to comment and review. The CPUC created a DRP roadmap and workshop, 
to inform the "scoping memo" later issued in 2016. 
In the scoping memo, the CPUC removed the three small and multi-jurisdictional investor-
owned utilities' applications from the proceeding so that they may be treated separately as a 
packaged group. This action was due to the distinct issues affecting this group. The CPUC 
consolidated the three large-utility DRP applications with the on-going DRP rulemaking. This 
allowed the CPUC to consider policy issues for rulemaking and more detailed technical issues more 
appropriate for the applications. The proceeding was divided into three separate tracks to run 
concurrently: 
? Pursuant to Pub. Util. Code Section 769, "distributed energy resources" means distributed renewable 
generation resources, energy efficiency, energy storage, electric vehicles, and demand response technologies. 
3 There are also three smaller investor-owned utilities: Bear Valley, Liberty and PacificCorp. These smaller 
investor-owned utilities were separated out of these rulemaking decisions. 
4 These plans propose a method of planning and supplying electrical use demands integrating DERs within the 
supply chain. 


File No. A-18-082 
Page No. 3 
• 
Track 1 Methodological Issues (quasi-legislative): To establish methodologies related to the 
electric distribution grid's interconnection capacity to absorb more DERs, and the value of 
DERs to the local grid ("Location Net Benefits Analysis"). The methodologies are to be 
developed following a series of workshops, demonstration projects and comment periods. 
• 
Track 2 Demonstration and Pilot Projects (ratesetting): To consider pilot projects to test the 
ability of DERs to provide various values. Each investor-owned utility proposed distinct 
pilot projects, which were evaluated, and approved or rejected individually. The CPUC 
categorized this as "ratesetting" because it would use this track to identify and authorize 
funding to conduct the demonstration projects. Decisions resolving the proposed pilot 
projects were issued in February and June of 2017. 
• 
Track 3 Policy Issues (quasi-legislative): To consider the implementation of ongoing energy 
forecasting and DER solicitation processes that would apply to the large electric investor-
owned utilities consolidated in the proceeding. 
Some methodology issues from Track l remain outstanding. For example, in 2017, the 
CPUC resolved particular issues related to the Local Net Benefits Analysis, but additional issues 
have come to light which require refinement of the earlier determinations. You wish to know if you 
would be prohibited under the permanent ban from providing advice or analysis to parties to this 
proceeding if your work is limited to the generally applicable methodologies and processes 
considered in the quasi-legislative Tracks l and 3. Specifically, you are currently interested in 
consulting work with the California Solar and Storage Association. This Solar Association wishes 
to make comments to the CPUC related to the Local Net Benefits Analysis refinements. You would 
prepare comments for the Solar Association in response to the CPUC's•recent ruling on the Local 
Net Benefits Analysis Working Group Refinement Final Report and investor-owned utilities' Local 
Net Benefits Analysis cost-effectiveness use case proposals. These issues will be resolved in a 
forthcoming CPUC decision that will modify the Local Net Benefits Analysis methodologies. 
R11/e111aki11g 14-10-003: To Create a Consistent Reg11/atory Fra111eworkfor the Guida11ce, 
Plan11i11g and Eva/11ation of /11tegrated Distrib11ted Energy Reso11rces ("IDER''). 
This proceeding was opened to create a regulatory framework for guidance, planning and 
evaluation of integrated DERs, and described as "ratesetting, generally applicable to the three large 
IOUs [investor-owned utilities]." Three scoping rulings that have been issued in the proceeding on 
issues that are largely methodological and process-related. Originally, the proceeding was divided 
into Phase I (quasi-legislative) and Phase 2 (ratesetting). A 2016 scoping ruling combined the 
phases and categorized this proceeding as "ratesetting" due to the consideration of pilot projects to 
test a shareholder incentive mechanism. However, you indicate that this proceeding to date has 
focused on the "big picture" and is more accurately seen as one of general policy and methodology. 
The scope of this proceeding is: 
• 
Development of a competitive solicitation framework; and continued development of 
technology-neutral cost-effectiveness methods and protocols; 


• 
Leveraging the demonstration projects in rulemaking 14-08-013; 
File No. A-18-082 
Page No. 4 
• 
The utility role, business models, and financial interests regarding DER deployment. 
• 
Design alternative sourcing mechanisms or approaches to satisfy distribution planning 
objectives; 
• 
Consider how existing programs, incentives, and tariffs can be coordinated to maximize 
locational benefits and minimize costs of DERs. 
You wish to know if you are prohibited under the permanent ban from providing advice or 
analysis to parties to this proceeding. Specifically, you envision working on smaller aspects of this 
proceeding, such as assisting the Solar Association with its comments related to the use of tariffs 
and the methodologies. You indicate that the proceeding did not previously address the use of 
tariffs with any specificity. 
ANALYSIS 
Public officials who have left state service are subject to two types of post-governmental 
employment provisions under the Act: the one-year ban and the permanent ban. (Sections 87400-
87406 and Regulation 18746.1.) Your question relates to the application of the permanent ban; 
however, we note that you are subject to the one-year ban until February 2019, based on your last 
date of state service, and may not make appearances or communications with your former agency 
during this time period. 5 
The permanent ban prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides" and 
participating for compensation in certain proceedings involving the State of California and other 
specific parties, if the proceeding is one in which the former state employee participated while 
employed by the state. (Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation 18741.1.) 
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or informal appearance or any 
oral or written communication made with the intent to influence a judicial, quasi-judicial, or other 
proceeding in which you participated while serving as a state administrative official. (Section 
87401.) This definition does not include proceedings that do not involve specific parties, such as the 
making of policies or rules of general application. (Black Advice Letter, No. A-98-320 and Beale 
Advice Letter, No. A-00-146.) For example, we have previously advised that a state administrative 
official who developed a system for setting foster care rates applicable to all group homes was not 
5 The "one-year ban" prohibits a former state employee from making, for compensation, any formal or 
informal appearance, or making any oral or written communication, before his or her former agency for the purpose of 
influencing any administrative or legislative actions or any discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, 
awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (See 
Section 87406; Regulation l 8746. l .) The ban applies for twelve months from the date the employee permanently leaves 
state office or employment. Although potentially prohibited under the permanent ban, assisting or advising clients or 
employers who might appear before or communicate with the official's former agency is not a prohibited appearance or 
communication under the one-year ban so long as the former official is not identified in connection with the appearance 
or communication. For more information please see the Commission's Leaving State Employment fact sheet: 
http://www.fppc.ca.gov/content/dam/fppc/NS-
OocumentsffAO/Public%200fficials%20and%20Ernployees/Leaving State Emplowentpdf. 


File No. A-18-082 
Page No. 5 
involved in a ''judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" because the system did not involve any 
one specific group home. (Bersinger Advice Letter, A-82-209.) In addition, we have advised that 
the fonnulation of a rate design policy for the CPUC was not a ''judicial, quasi-judicial or other 
proceeding" because the policy had an industry-wide application that did not focus on any specific 
utility. (Fong Advice Letter, A-88-024.) 
An official is considered to have "participated" in a proceeding if he or she took part in the 
proceeding "personally, and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, fonnal written 
recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of confidential 
infonnation . ... " (Section 87400(d).) 
DRPs Ru/emal..ing 14-08-013: 
As the Energy Policy Adviser to the CPUC president, you were actively involved in the 
DRP proceeding. This proceeding involved a mix of fonnulating general methodologies and 
policies, as well as rulings related to particular parties, such as the Track 2 pilot project 
determinations. To the extent that your consultant work is limited to the quasi-legislative 
methodologies and policies that are of general application, you are not prohibited by the pennanent 
ban from providing comments to the Solar Association. The pennanent ban does bar your 
participation in matters that do not apply equally to the class of utilities such as rulings that related 
to particular parties, to the extent that you previously participated in that aspect of the rulemaking 
proceeding. Also, please keep in mind that the one-year ban applies to you and prohibits certain 
communications between you and your fonner employer, the CPUC, until February 2019. 
IDER Rulemaking /4-10-003 
You were also actively involved in the IDER proceeding. Despite its designation as 
"ratesetting," you state that this proceeding related to. general policy and methodology applicable to 
large investor-owned utilities equally. To the extent that your consultant work is limited to the 
quasi-legislative methodological issues and policy issues that are of general application, you are not 
prohibited by the permanent ban from providing advice or analysis to the Solar Association. 
Again, please keep in mind that the one-year ban applies to you and prohibits certain 
communications between you and your fonner employer, the CPUC, until February 2019. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
LKH:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
;;z;t·~z 
By: 
L. Karen Harrison 
Counsel, Legal Division

To the extent that these proceedings focused on the quasi-legislative matters of establishing

Consider how existing programs, incentives, and tariffs can be coordinated to maximize 
locational benefits and minimize costs of DERs. 
You wish to know if you are prohibited under the permanent ban from providing advice or 
analysis to parties to this proceeding. Specifically, you envision working on smaller aspects of this 
proceeding, such as assisting the Solar Association with its comments related to the use of tariffs 
and the methodologies. You indicate that the proceeding did not previously address the use of 
tariffs with any specificity.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION • 1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 1/ (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 Scott Murtishaw 1355 Berkeley Way Berkeley, CA 94702 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-18-082 Dear Mr. Murtishaw: June 5, 2018 This letter responds to your request for advice regarding post-government employment provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section I 090. Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby ( 1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. QUESTION As the former Energy Policy Adviser to the president of the California Public Utilities Commission ("CPUC"), are you prohibited under the permanent ban from acting as a consultant on matters related to the proceedings on Distribution Resource Plans (Rulemaking 14-08-013 ), and Integrated Distributed Energy Resources (Ruiemaking 14-10-003 )? CONCLUSION To the extent that these proceedings focused on the quasi-legislative matters of establishing policies, rules and methodologies of general application to the class of large electric investor-owned utilities, you are not barred by the permanent ban from consultant work related to these proceedings. The permanent ban does bar your participation in matters that do not apply equally to the class of utilities, to the extent you previously participated in that aspect of the rulemaking proceeding. 1 The Polilical Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. FACTS AS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTOR File No. A-18-082 Page No. 2 You were formerly employed as Energy Policy Adviser to the president of the California Public Utilities Commission ("CPUC") from July 2010 until January 2018. While at the CPUC you worked on two rulemaking proceedings related to the relatively new area of Distributed Energy Resources" ("DERs").2 The two rulemaking proceedings have some overlap, and together work to ensure that cost-effective DERs are integrated by large investor-owned utility companies into the electric system's operations and distribution planning as an alternative to investments in traditional forms of electricity generation. Both proceedings addressed policy issues and methodology development, and you state that these proceedings are equally applicable to each member of the class of large electric investor- owned utilities. The large investor-owned utilities are: Pacific Gas and Electric, Southern California Edison and San Diego Gas and Electric.3 Public Utilities
